ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken burger / chicken sandwich — fast-food type, fried chicken patty in bun
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (burger/sandwich composite) used as closest defensible match; no dedicated spicy fried chicken sandwich code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT food concept for spicy fried chicken sandwich identified; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard fast-food spicy fried chicken sandwich weighing approximately 200 g, comprising a deep-fried spiced chicken patty, a soft refined-wheat bun, spicy mayonnaise sauce, lettuce, and pickles.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken Sandwich, Fast Food (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fast-food chicken sandwich entries (FDC ID references: fried chicken sandwich composites in SR Legacy). The input record's per-100g values (energy 172.73 kcal, protein 6.36 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 15.45 g) are consistent with USDA fast-food chicken sandwich data and are retained with medium confidence. Spicy variant is assumed to have the same macronutrient profile as the standard fried chicken sandwich with minor sodium adjustment from spicy sauce; no separate USDA entry for spicy variant exists. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not separately reported in the primary source. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and remaining micronutrients not reported in the input are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — White bread / refined wheat bun composite analogue; fast-food chicken sandwich GI estimated from refined-wheat bun dominant carbohydrate fraction.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a spicy fried chicken sandwich in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 66 is estimated using the refined white-wheat burger bun as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle (GI ~70 for white bread; adjusted downward to ~66 for mixed sandwich context with protein and fat moderating glycaemic response), consistent with methodology in Atkinson et al. 2008. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (66 × 30.9) / 100 ≈ 20.4, using 200 g serving with 15.45 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (327 mg/100 g) from fried coating, spicy sauce, and pickles, with trans fat (0.09 g/100 g) contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this sandwich due to its elevated sodium load (approximately 654 mg per 200 g serving) and trans fat content; if consumed, request no pickles, reduce sauce quantity, and pair with a potassium-rich side such as a banana or salad to partially offset sodium burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI refined wheat bun (estimated GI 66) delivering a glycemic load of approximately 20 per serving, combined with low dietary fibre (0.91 g/100 g) and added sugar in spicy sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat this sandwich as an occasional food only; the medium-to-high glycaemic load (~20 per serving) and minimal fibre content will produce a significant postprandial glucose rise, and the spicy mayonnaise sauce likely contains added sugars — portion control and pairing with a non-starchy vegetable side are strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Trans fat (0.09 g/100 g; ~0.18 g per serving), saturated fat (2.18 g/100 g), high sodium, and deep-frying-generated advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this sandwich; the combination of trans fat, saturated fat, high sodium (~654 mg per serving), and AGEs from deep-frying represents a multi-pathway cardiovascular risk burden — a grilled chicken option with a wholegrain bun is a substantially safer alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying produces pro-inflammatory AGEs; trans fat (0.09 g/100 g) is a hard RA disqualifier; spicy chilli-based sauce may trigger individual flare sensitivity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this sandwich; deep-frying and trans fat are both established pro-inflammatory disqualifiers for RA, and the spicy chilli component may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals — a grilled chicken option without spicy sauce is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Spicy Fried Chicken Sandwich is a fast-food style sandwich widely available in Singapore through delivery platforms and quick-service restaurants. It consists of a deep-fried, spiced chicken patty — typically marinated in chilli-based seasoning and coated in a seasoned flour batter — served inside a soft refined-wheat burger bun. The sandwich is dressed with a creamy spicy mayonnaise or sriracha-style sauce, crisp lettuce leaves, and sliced pickles. The chicken patty delivers a crunchy exterior with a juicy, mildly spicy interior; the overall flavour profile is savoury, tangy, and moderately hot. Per 100 g, the sandwich provides approximately 173 kcal, 6.4 g protein, 10.0 g fat (including 2.2 g saturated fat and 0.09 g trans fat), and 15.5 g carbohydrate with only 0.9 g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 327 mg/100 g, reflecting contributions from the fried coating, spicy sauce, and pickles. The glycaemic index is estimated at 66 (medium), driven by the refined wheat bun, yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 20 per 200 g serving. Clinically, this sandwich is a high-concern food for hypertension (high sodium and trans fat), Type 2 Diabetes (medium-high GL, low fibre, added sugars in sauce), cardiovascular disease (trans fat, saturated fat, AGEs from deep-frying), and rheumatoid arthritis (AGEs and trans fat are pro-inflammatory disqualifiers). It is classified as halal and is a common lunch or dinner item in Singapore's fast-food landscape.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fast-food chicken sandwich composites, which are the closest approved-source match. No exact USDA entry for a spicy fried chicken sandwich exists; values from the input record are consistent with USDA fast-food chicken sandwich data and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact approved-source entry for this specific spicy variant was identified.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation for this fast-food composite dish is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fast-food chicken sandwich entries as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values were not reported in the input record and are not available for this specific composite dish in the Singapore HPB database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 3. trans_fat_g of 0.09 g/100 g is retained from the input record; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with residual trans fat from deep-frying in partially hydrogenated oils or natural ruminant trans fat traces in the chicken. 4. Two portions are noted in the input: 1 chicken sandwich (200 g, default) and 1 set meal including sandwich, side, and drink (550 g); the 200 g sandwich-only portion is used as the canonical serving for nutrient calculations. The set meal portion is not used for per-serving calculations as it includes items (side dish, beverage) not reflected in the per-100g nutrient profile. 5. The input record's health_suitability referenced a soft drink as a disqualifier; this is specific to the set meal context and is not applicable to the sandwich-only record. Health context in this output is based on the sandwich alone. 6. GI estimated at 66 using analogous refined-wheat bun dominant carbohydrate fraction per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
